evidence: Both files are declarative contract catalogs only (constants + catalog entry builders) and introduce no imports, no init() hooks, and no import-time side effects.
Contract identities are centralized in these two files (`protocol.actions.*` and `startram.errors.*`), which keeps boundary naming predictable at package level.

Each contract repeats identity fields across multiple literals (ID constant, descriptor name string, description/message fields), creating manual synchronization points that can drift from external consumers/registrations in other modules.
cross_module_architecture.issues_preventing_higher_score: The catalog definitions rely on repeated string literals and parallel fields without a stronger typed builder/schema contract, so cross-module contract drift remains possible even though current local structure is clean.

evidence: Multiple modules use large callback-bag runtimes with manual defaulting/validation (`importerRuntime`, `startupInitRuntime`, `RectifyRuntime`) where significant code is wiring rather than domain behavior.
Mutation contracts are intentionally untyped (`mutateFn any`) across orchestration/workflow/config section updaters, and some mutation paths silently no-op on type mismatch instead of failing fast.

Runtime facade structs in orchestration duplicate each other (`RuntimeStartupOps` vs `RuntimeConfigOps` + `RuntimeLoadOps` + `RuntimeServiceOps`) and are reassembled through pass-through constructors, increasing call-surface area without clear policy gain.

README.md claims contract governance coverage and references targeted contract tests, but the listed targeted gate examples are upload/startram-focused; no explicit C2C-focused test target is named despite `C2CConnectAction` being part of active protocol contracts in `goseg/protocol/contracts/protocol_contracts.go`.

README.md states compatibility descriptors and lifecycle assertions should be enforced through `contracts_test.go`, yet the reviewed contract source files contain no local guardrails or explicit linkage for C2C lifecycle verification, making the strategy depend on external process alignment.

`goseg/protocol/contracts/startram_contracts.go` defines StarTram error contract metadata, while README quality-gate text mixes policy and enforcement details; this docs-driven coupling increases risk that added contract families/actions are not proportionally represented in gate coverage.

Section/domain metadata is maintained in multiple parallel registries: `SysConfigSectionDescriptorList` in `goseg/structs/configs.go` and `sysConfigSectionPatchSpecs` in `goseg/config/config_update_registry.go`, which must remain manually synchronized.

Mutation APIs rely on `any` with runtime casting across config systems (`UpdateSection` in `goseg/structs/configs.go`, `UrbitSectionMutator` in `goseg/config/urbit.go`), creating inconsistent behavior and hidden coupling.

Workflow runtime files combine command routing, validation, persistence, container lifecycle orchestration, and transition event emission in the same module (`goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go`, `goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go`).

Runtime seam composition is highly fragmented with overlapping operation bundles and duplicate default constructors in `goseg/docker/orchestration/runtime_ops.go`, reflected by heavy callback-wiring scaffolding in `goseg/startuporchestrator/startup_orchestrator_test.go`.

In `goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go`, `SvcDeleteFn` failures during endpoint/cancel cleanup are logged but not returned, while adjacent operations in the same transition path return wrapped errors and fail the transition.

In `goseg/importer/importer.go`, token-validation failures in `OpenUploadEndpoint` return generic `errors.New(...)` strings (`token mismatch`, `upload token id is not authorized`), but `validateUploadRequest` returns different wrapped/contextual errors for the same auth domain.

`goseg/docker/events/events.go` defines explicit publish failure contracts (`ErrTransitionPublishTimeout`, `ErrTransitionBusFull`), while `goseg/session/logstream_session_store.go` publishes via `messageBus <- logData` with no error return and blocking semantics.

findings.summary: Large callback-bag runtime structs create high indirection cost and duplicated wiring logic across subsystems.
evidence: `importerRuntime` declares many function seams spanning unrelated concerns (upload, config, provisioning) and `withDefaultsImporterRuntime` manually copies each callback.
`startupInitRuntime` and `RectifyRuntime` repeat the same pattern (`withDefaults...`, `validate`) instead of reusing a smaller stable dependency interface per workflow.
`bootstrapRuntime` in `main.go` is another callback bag with pass-through methods (`bootstrap`, `startServer`, `runC2cCheck`) that mostly add nil checks and forwarding.
findings.suggestion: Split callback bags into smaller role-specific interfaces (for example config-store, container-ops, transition-publisher), inject concrete dependencies once via constructors, and remove repetitive per-field defaulting layers in favor of explicit composition at module boundaries.

findings.summary: Core mutation abstractions rely on `any` callbacks, weakening interface honesty and making misuse silently possible.
evidence: `RuntimeUrbitOps.UpdateUrbitSectionFn` uses `mutateFn any`, and `workflowRuntime.UpdateUrbitSectionFn` mirrors it with the same untyped contract.
`shipworkflow` forwards typed mutators through `any` casting (`any(mutate)`), so compile-time guarantees are lost at the boundary.
`SysConfig.UpdateSection` dispatches through descriptor mutations that type-assert and otherwise do nothing, so wrong callback shapes can fail silently.
findings.suggestion: Replace `any` mutation seams with typed contracts (generic section updater or explicit per-section methods), and return an explicit error when a mutation callback type is invalid instead of silently ignoring it.

findings.summary: Orchestration runtime facades overlap heavily and are recomposed through pass-through helpers, adding layering without behavioral leverage.
evidence: `RuntimeStartupOps` duplicates fields already represented in `RuntimeConfigOps`, `RuntimeLoadOps`, and `RuntimeServiceOps`, then `defaultRuntimeStartupOps` re-copies those fields.
`RuntimeTransitionOpsFromContainerOps` and `RuntimeTransitionOpsFromUrbitOps` are thin merge helpers around embedded structs rather than policy-bearing abstractions.
Downstream runtimes (`workflowRuntime`, `RectifyRuntime`) consume subsets of these ops, indicating the top-level facades are broader than actual use sites.
findings.suggestion: Flatten orchestration runtime API into a smaller set of cohesive capability interfaces aligned to consumers (workflow, rectify, startup) and remove duplicate aggregate facades that only repackage the same callbacks.

findings.summary: Package-level mutable seam variables act as hidden dependency injection, creating implicit abstraction boundaries and harder-to-reason wiring.
evidence: Startup uses package-level function vars (`startupUpdateSettingsSnapshot`, `startupStartramSettingsSnapshot`, `startupSwapSettingsSnapshot`) as dependency seams.
Main similarly uses mutable package vars (`runtimeContextSnapshotFn`, `connectivitySettingsSnapshot`, `netCheckFn`, `debugModeFn`) that influence runtime behavior outside constructor signatures.

Importer has package-level dependency function vars (`resolveStoragePathFor`, `importerSwapSettingsFor`, `importerStartramSettingsFor`) parallel to explicit runtime structs, resulting in mixed injection styles.
findings.suggestion: Standardize on constructor-injected dependencies and remove mutable package-level seam vars for runtime behavior; keep package-level vars only for constants and immutable defaults.

findings.summary: Contract families use duplicated manual catalog wiring instead of one extensible registration boundary.
evidence: `protocol_contracts.go` and `startram_contracts.go` both define parallel `*CatalogSpecs`, `*CatalogEntries`, and `*CatalogEntriesSnapshot` flows with family-specific constants and manual entry assembly.
README describes governance via shared contract APIs, but reviewed family files still implement repetitive family-specific registration patterns that can drift independently.
findings.suggestion: Introduce a single contract-family registration helper (or data-driven registry declaration) in the shared contracts boundary and migrate upload/C2C/StarTram families to that mechanism so new families only provide declarative entries, not custom catalog plumbing.

findings.summary: Quality-gate policy does not show explicit C2C contract verification for active C2C contract entries.
evidence: README CI section names targeted tests for upload and StarTram semantics, but does not enumerate an explicit C2C contract test path.
`protocol_contracts.go` includes active C2C contract metadata (`C2CConnectAction`), so omission of explicit C2C-targeted verification in documented gates creates a strategy blind spot.
findings.suggestion: Extend the runtime contract gate and documented targeted test matrix to include explicit C2C contract assertions (including lifecycle/compatibility checks), and keep that mapping in one authoritative contract-governance table so policy and enforcement cannot diverge.

findings.summary: Config section contracts are duplicated across independent registries, creating structural drift risk.
evidence: `goseg/structs/configs.go` defines canonical section IDs and descriptor registry (`SysConfigSectionDescriptorList`, `SysConfigSectionDescriptors`).

`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go` separately defines `sysConfigSectionPatchSpecs` with the same section set and then conditionally skips unknown sections in `confPatchServices()` (`if !ok { continue }`).
`goseg/config/config_update_schema.go` builds patch-key registries independently at init-time, adding another place where section/field coverage must stay in lockstep.
findings.suggestion: Define one authoritative section catalog (section id + typed accessor + patch field builders) and derive descriptors, patch services, and key registries from it; fail fast on missing section mappings instead of silently skipping.

findings.summary: Section mutation relies on `any` + runtime casts with inconsistent failure semantics across config domains.
evidence: In `goseg/structs/configs.go`, section `mutate` callbacks use type assertions and silently no-op when the function type does not match (`if ok { ... }`, no error path).
In `goseg/config/urbit.go`, `UrbitSectionMutator` also uses `any` and runtime casting but returns explicit type errors (`section %s expects %s`).
`goseg/config/config_update_registry.go` bridges into this system through generic wrappers (`applySectionPatchValue`) that still dispatch into runtime-typed section mutation.
findings.suggestion: Unify mutation contracts behind typed interfaces/generics that return explicit errors for all section/type mismatches, and remove silent no-op mutation paths from section descriptors.

findings.summary: Ship workflow runtime modules mix domain decisions, infrastructure actions, and transition plumbing in the same functions.
evidence: `goseg/shipworkflow/urbit_operations_runtime.go` combines command routing, alias/domain validation, config persistence, click/startram API calls, docker lifecycle operations, and transition-event wiring in one file (e.g., `buildMinIODomainSteps`, `buildToggleMinIOLinkSteps`, `schedulePack`).

`goseg/shipworkflow/startram_lifecycle_runtime.go` similarly combines policy branching (`settings.WgOn/WgRegistered`), transition event templates, config updates, container operations, and service deletion side effects in single execution paths (`runStartramToggleWithRuntime`, `runStartramEndpointWithRuntime`, `runStartramCancelWithRuntime`).
findings.suggestion: Split workflow handlers into: (1) pure transition planning/domain policy, (2) side-effect executors (docker/startram/config adapters), and (3) event emission wrappers; keep each transition step focused on one responsibility.

findings.summary: WiFi runtime service methods expose inconsistent object invariants and nil-safety expectations.
evidence: `goseg/system/wifi_runtime_api.go` defends nil receiver/state in some methods (`ConstructWifiInfo`, `IsWifiEnabled`, `ToggleDevice`) but other public methods dereference directly (`StartWiFiInfoLoop`, `StopWiFiInfoLoop`, `ConnectToWifi`, `DisconnectWifi`).

`goseg/system/wifi_test.go` explicitly documents nil-safe behavior expectation for receiver in `TestConstructWifiInfoNilReceiverNoPanic`, indicating a service-level contract that is only partially applied.
findings.suggestion: Choose one explicit service invariant (constructor-required non-nil instance vs nil-safe no-op/error API) and enforce it consistently across all public methods with shared guard/normalization helpers.

findings.summary: Protocol/action contract coherence checks are reimplemented in multiple test modules instead of using a shared matrix harness.
evidence: `goseg/protocol/actions/actions_test.go` repeatedly validates action metadata/order/compatibility and descriptor parity.
`goseg/protocol/contracts/contracts_test.go` separately validates binding validity, ordering, and descriptor presence.
`goseg/uploadsvc/service_test.go` repeats supported-action parity/dispatch-table coverage against the same action contract surface.
findings.suggestion: Create a shared contract conformance test helper that consumes one canonical action-contract matrix and reuse it across protocol and service tests to reduce drift and maintenance duplication.

findings.summary: Startram transition cleanup logs delete failures but still reports success
evidence: In `runStartramEndpointWithRuntime`, failures from `SvcDeleteFn` for urbit/s3 anchors are logged (`zap.L().Error(...)`) and execution continues with `return nil` from the step.

In `runStartramCancelWithRuntime`, reset-mode service deletions also log-and-continue, but neighboring operations (`CancelSub`, `CycleWgKeyFn`) convert errors into returned `fmt.Errorf(...)`, creating mixed failure behavior in one workflow.
findings.suggestion: Make cleanup failure policy explicit and uniform: either aggregate cleanup errors with `errors.Join` and return them so the transition fails, or route all cleanup failures through a single best-effort helper that emits a typed non-fatal error event and marks the transition as degraded.

findings.summary: Importer auth paths emit incompatible error contracts for similar token failures
evidence: `OpenUploadEndpoint` returns plain string errors for auth failures (`errors.New("token mismatch")`, `errors.New("upload token id is not authorized")`).

`validateUploadRequest` in the same module returns different messages and wraps callback errors (`fmt.Errorf("upload token validation failed: %w", authz.AuthorizationErr)`) for equivalent authorization failures, forcing callers/tests to branch on message text rather than stable typed errors.
findings.suggestion: Introduce typed/sentinel auth errors for importer token validation (e.g., token mismatch, unauthorized token, callback missing) and return them consistently from both endpoint-open and upload-validate paths, with `%w` wrapping only for lower-level causes.

findings.summary: Event publication uses conflicting failure semantics across transition and log buses
evidence: `EventRuntime.publishDropOnFull` returns explicit publish errors for timeout/full bus conditions, giving callers a predictable failure contract.
`logstreamSessionStore.PublishSystemLog` writes directly to `messageBus <- logData` without context or error return; under pressure it can block producers and offers no failure signal to callers.
`LogstreamRuntime` consumers in `logs.go` are built around processing from this channel, but producers cannot observe/drop/report backpressure in the same way transition publishers can.
findings.suggestion: Unify publish APIs: add `PublishSystemLog(ctx, data) error` with non-blocking/backpressure behavior aligned to transition bus semantics (`timeout/full` typed errors), then update log producers/consumers to handle these errors consistently.

findings.summary: Subsystems rely on mutable package-level singletons, creating hidden coupling and non-local side effects.
evidence: `defaultBroadcastStateRuntime` is a mutable global broadcast store used as implicit default runtime.
`activeSessionBoundary` is globally swapped state for auth session behavior.
`runtimeContext` is global process context initialized in `init()` and mutated via setters.
findings.suggestion: Move singleton mutation behind explicit application bootstrap wiring: construct runtimes once, inject them into subsystems, and remove package-level mutable defaults from public execution paths.

findings.summary: Operationally significant failures are logged and suppressed, producing inconsistent failure semantics.
evidence: `LoadMinIOsWithRuntime` logs start failures per pier and continues, then returns nil.
Broadcast congestion drops updates after retries and only emits a warning.
Llama stop failure is logged as warning and startup proceeds, while similar runtime operations elsewhere return hard errors.
findings.suggestion: Define a subsystem-wide partial-failure contract (aggregate error type with per-target failures) and return it to callers; reserve log-only behavior for explicitly documented non-critical paths.

findings.summary: Some missing callback dependencies incorrectly return success, masking misconfiguration.
evidence: `waitForContainerRunningWithRuntime` returns nil immediately when required callbacks are missing.
`ensureLlamaVolumesWithRuntime` returns nil when volume callbacks are missing.
findings.suggestion: Fail closed on missing required callbacks by returning explicit dependency errors, and make optional callbacks opt-in via separate code paths with clear naming.

findings.summary: Facade layers add call depth but little policy/value translation across docker orchestration boundaries.
evidence: Top-level docker functions mostly forward directly to orchestration with identical signatures.
Orchestration minio/llama wrappers then forward directly into container package functions.
Repeated runtime construction plus forwarding appears across many entrypoints without central policy handling.
findings.suggestion: Collapse pass-through chains into a single explicit boundary: either keep one façade with real policy/telemetry/error normalization, or export container/orchestration entrypoints directly and remove redundant wrappers.

findings.summary: Access point router flow combines unrelated concerns and contains inconsistent command/policy handling.
evidence: Router execution builds shell command strings via join logic while some calls pass prejoined command text and others pass tokenized args.
NAT section comments/reference imply wlan<->inet routing, but key iptables rules use `rt.Wlan` where outbound interface should reflect `rt.Inet`.
Lifecycle orchestration, command serialization, firewall mutation, and service process startup are tightly coupled in one module path.
findings.suggestion: Split router responsibilities into command builder, firewall policy planner, and process executor; switch to argument-safe execution API and add explicit interface-selection validation for NAT rule construction.
